anovapowersim 1.1.0.9000
  • Get started
    • Fixed-sample power
    • Power for unbalanced designs
    • Covariance and nonsphericity
    • Calculated power
    • Comparison with G\*Power
  • Reference
  • Changelog

Plan: Make multi-df power conditional on an explicit means pattern

Source: means_pattern_plan.md

Problem to solve

For a within-subject ANOVA term with more than one degree of freedom, an effect is a vector in a multidimensional contrast space. Under sphericity, rotating that vector within the tested term does not change the omnibus F-test’s power: the effect’s magnitude is sufficient. Under nonsphericity, the covariance in the contrast space is not rotationally symmetric. Power can therefore depend on how the effect vector aligns with the covariance structure.

Consequently, for a multi-df within-subject term with population Greenhouse–Geisser epsilon below 1, target_pes and the covariance matrix do not uniquely determine simulation power. Different relative cell-mean shapes can have the same target_pes and covariance, yet produce materially different power.

The balanced simulation functions currently hide this choice. default_term_pattern() constructs one particular contrast-space direction, and calibrate_design_means() scales it to the requested target_pes. power_sim is therefore valid for that direction, but the direction is neither chosen by the user nor made explicit. The calculated noncentral-F result is a direction-insensitive approximation and remains unchanged by the selected pattern.

The apparent paradox is that target_pes and the complete covariance can be identical while power differs. Write the tested mean component as the contrast-space vector theta and its covariance as Psi. The scalar noncentral-F approximation uses an aggregate signal-to-noise quantity related to ||theta||^2 / tr(Psi), which is unchanged when a fixed-length theta is rotated. Under nonsphericity, however, the actual univariate repeated-measures statistic has a weighted noncentral distribution. Its power depends on how the components of theta align with the eigenspaces of Psi, not only on the aggregate scalar noncentrality. This affects simulated power for both the GG-corrected paths (ss_type = "II"/"III") and the uncorrected ss_type = "I" path. GG correction changes the reference degrees of freedom and rejection threshold; it does not create the underlying direction sensitivity.

This change will let users specify the relative cell-mean shape used by balanced simulations and will warn only when the package’s hidden default direction is consequential.

Intended behavior

  1. Add a readable, sparse means_pattern() constructor.
  2. Add an optional means_pattern = NULL argument to the balanced simulation-based APIs and to design_term_means().
  3. Resolve a supplied raw pattern into a complete cell pattern and project it deterministically onto the requested ANOVA term.
  4. Scale that projected pattern by one positive constant to reach target_pes, using the existing calibration machinery.
  5. Replace the current internal default direction with a normalized, deterministic linear/Kronecker convention.
  6. Warn when a balanced simulation uses that package-default direction for a multi-df, nonspherical within-subject term.
  7. Do not issue this warning when the user supplied a pattern, for calculation-only functions, or for power_unbalanced().

The affected simulation APIs are experimental, and the released behavior was effectively pattern-agnostic in the supported spherical cases. No legacy default-pattern option is required. The new default should be chosen for clarity and reproducibility rather than preservation of the current internal sum-contrast direction.

Public API

New constructor

Export:

means_pattern(...)

Keep the surface syntax parallel to the package’s other sparse-cell APIs: cell_counts() uses repeated factor assignments ending in n, cell_design() uses repeated factor assignments ending in n and m, and means_pattern() uses repeated factor assignments ending in value:

means_pattern(
  time = 1, value = 0,
  time = 2, value = 0.3,
  time = 3, value = 0.5,
  time = 4, value = 0.6
)

For an interaction:

means_pattern(
  group = 1, time = 3, value =  1,
  group = 2, time = 3, value = -1
)

Balanced designs store factor counts rather than user-defined level labels. To reduce the difference from cell_design(), accept either one-based integer indices or the package-generated balanced level names. These calls are equivalent:

means_pattern(time = 3, value = 1)
means_pattern(time = "time3", value = 1)

Generated names must match the levels in balanced_anova_design() exactly; arbitrary user labels are not available for count-based balanced designs. Resolve both input forms to the same canonical level index internally and detect duplicates again after canonicalization. Documentation should use one form consistently in each example and explicitly point out the alternative. Error messages should show both the valid indices and generated names. value is reserved and must be a finite numeric scalar.

The constructor is deliberately sparse:

  • Cells not listed by the user have raw value zero.
  • Factors outside the tested term are not supplied and are broadcast during resolution.
  • If a referenced factor in the term is omitted from a row definition, that definition is broadcast across that factor. The resulting target-term projection must still be nonzero.
  • Every row must use the same factor names in the same order.
  • Duplicate cell definitions are errors, including definitions that identify the same cell once by index and once by generated name.
  • Factor names and level bounds are checked when the pattern is resolved against a design.

The returned object should have class anovapowersim_means_pattern and retain the sparse definitions. It is not term-aware until passed to a power function.

New arguments

Append means_pattern = NULL to:

  • power_curve()
  • power_n()
  • power_achieved()
  • power_sensitivity()
  • design_term_means()

Appending the argument avoids changing the meaning of existing positional calls.

Do not add it to:

  • power_n_calc()
  • power_achieved_calc()
  • power_sensitivity_calc()
  • power_unbalanced()

The calculation-only functions do not simulate a mean vector and continue to use the conventional epsilon-adjusted noncentral-F calculation. power_unbalanced() already receives literal cell means through cell_design(), so its effect direction is already specified.

Example calls

Within-subject main effect:

power_n(
  within = c(time = 4),
  term = "time",
  target_pes = 0.15,
  power = 0.90,
  means_pattern = means_pattern(
    time = 1, value = 0,
    time = 2, value = 0.3,
    time = 3, value = 0.5,
    time = 4, value = 0.6
  ),
  covariance = within_covariance(
    sd = 1,
    correlations = c("time1:time3" = 0.3)
  )
)

Interaction with sparse input:

power_curve(
  between = c(group = 2),
  within = c(time = 4),
  term = "group:time",
  target_pes = 0.10,
  n_range = 40,
  means_pattern = means_pattern(
    group = 1, time = 3, value =  1,
    group = 2, time = 3, value = -1
  ),
  covariance = within_covariance(
    sd = 1,
    correlations = c("time1:time3" = 0.3)
  ),
  n_sims = 5000
)

Calibrated means only:

design_term_means(
  balanced_anova_design(within = c(time = 4)),
  term = "time",
  target_pes = 0.15,
  n = 30,
  means_pattern = means_pattern(
    time = 1, value = 0,
    time = 2, value = 0.3,
    time = 3, value = 0.5,
    time = 4, value = 0.6
  )
)

Pattern resolution and projection

Add an internal helper such as:

resolve_means_pattern(means_pattern, spec, term)

It should return NULL for a NULL input. Otherwise it should:

  1. Verify the object class and validate its sparse definitions.
  2. Reject factor names that are not part of term.
  3. Resolve each supplied factor value from either a one-based integer index or an exact package-generated level name; reject all other values and report both valid forms.
  4. Reject duplicate sparse cells after names and indices have been converted to their canonical level indices.
  5. Build the complete factorial grid for the factors in term.
  6. Fill unmentioned term cells with zero and apply any explicit sparse values.
  7. Broadcast the raw pattern across design factors outside term.
  8. Align the resulting vector with the same complete cell ordering used by default_term_pattern() and the balanced design matrices.
  9. Construct the sum-contrast model matrix for the complete balanced design.
  10. Select the model-matrix columns assigned to term.
  11. Project the raw pattern onto the column space for the requested term using deterministic linear algebra, such as a QR-based orthogonal projection.
  12. Error clearly if the projected component has effectively zero norm.
  13. Normalize the projected vector to unit Euclidean norm and reshape it to the package’s n_between_cells x n_within_cells means-matrix layout.

Projection is preferable to rejecting every pattern that also contains an intercept, main effect, or lower-order interaction. Sparse interaction patterns naturally contain such components. Projection retains the direction relevant to the requested omnibus term and removes the irrelevant components exactly, without relying on a simulated reference dataset or approximate fitted-term checks.

For example, the sparse group:time pattern with values 1 and -1 at two cells also induces a group marginal effect before projection. It should remain valid: the resolver extracts its pure group:time component.

The error condition should refer to a zero target-term projection, not merely an all-zero raw pattern. A raw pattern may vary across cells while containing no component of the requested interaction.

Meaning of pattern values

Documentation must explain that the supplied values describe a relative shape, not an effect size:

  • The projected pattern is uniformly rescaled to reach target_pes.
  • Multiplying all raw values by the same positive constant produces the same calibrated result.
  • Reversing the sign of the entire pattern produces the same omnibus F-test power.
  • Adding an intercept or lower-order effect does not change the resolved target-term component.
  • Relative direction within the target term’s multidimensional contrast space is what can change power under nonsphericity.
  • Omitted sparse cells have raw value zero, but projection can make the final target-term pattern nonzero in cells the user did not list.

Package-default direction

Replace the current equal-weight sum of contr.sum columns with a normalized linear/Kronecker convention when means_pattern = NULL.

For a factor with L levels, define its centered linear score vector as:

c_L[i] = i - (L + 1) / 2,  i = 1, ..., L

Normalize c_L to unit Euclidean norm. Before normalization, examples are:

2 levels: (-1,  1)
3 levels: (-1,  0,  1)
4 levels: (-3, -1,  1,  3)
5 levels: (-2, -1,  0,  1,  2)

For an interaction, form the outer/Kronecker product of the normalized component vectors for every factor in the term. Equivalently, the raw cell score is the product of its factors’ centered linear scores. For a 2-by-2 interaction, the unnormalized pattern is:

+1  -1
-1  +1

This is the canonical 2-by-2 interaction direction; that interaction has only one degree of freedom. For a 2-by-4 interaction, the unnormalized pattern is:

+3  +1  -1  -3
-3  -1  +1  +3

For a higher-order interaction, use the Kronecker product across every component factor. Because the component vectors are unit norm, their Kronecker product is also unit norm. After broadcasting across factors outside the tested term and aligning to the complete design grid, normalize the final target-term matrix once more to protect against ordering or replication details.

This default is a clean, deterministic convention, not a scientifically neutral pattern. It is readily interpretable for ordered factors such as time, dose, or session, but an unordered multi-level factor has no nonzero direction that is invariant to every permutation of its levels. Explicit means_pattern() input should therefore remain the recommendation whenever the expected shape is known, especially for nominal factors and multi-df nonspherical terms.

Document the generated factor-level ordering used to construct the linear scores. The constructor help, covariance vignette, and direction warning should link this definition to the reason a user may want to override it. Do not describe the default as neutral, representative, or uniquely implied by target_pes.

No legacy preset is needed. The current direction is internal to experimental development functionality, while released supported cases were effectively pattern-agnostic. Remove documentation and tests that assume the old (1, 1, 1, -3) four-level direction.

Calibration plumbing

Balanced setup

Thread means_pattern through prepare_power_curve_inputs() and prepare_balanced_power_inputs().

Resolve and project it once in prepare_balanced_power_inputs(), after spec and term have been resolved. Store the resulting normalized matrix internally in the setup object, for example as setup$means_pattern. Also store:

custom_means_pattern = !is.null(means_pattern)

The resolved matrix only needs to live in the setup while calculations are running. It does not need to be added to the returned public object.

Mean calibration

Extend calibrate_design_means() with an optional resolved base-pattern argument. Its selection becomes conceptually:

base <- if (is.null(resolved_means_pattern)) {
  default_linear_term_pattern(spec, term)
} else {
  resolved_means_pattern
}

The helper may retain the internal name default_term_pattern() if changing it would avoid unnecessary plumbing, but its implementation and tests must use the normalized linear/Kronecker algorithm described above.

The existing empirical reference fit, calibration_pes_for_ncp(), and compute_scale_factor() then scale that base pattern to target_pes. No direction-specific changes are needed in compute_scale_factor().

Thread the resolved pattern through run_design_power_at_n() and every run_one() closure in the four simulation APIs. power_sensitivity() must use the same resolved relative pattern at every candidate target_pes.

Extend design_term_means() through the same resolver and calibration path.

Returned objects and printing

Add only the boolean metadata:

custom_means_pattern = !is.null(means_pattern)

to balanced simulation result objects. Update their print and summary methods to show one compact line:

means pattern: default linear/Kronecker

or:

means pattern: custom

Do not expose the resolved pattern matrix as a public result field in this pass.

Direction-sensitivity warning

Factor the repeated within-term degree-of-freedom calculation into one internal helper, for example:

within_term_df(spec, term)

Reuse it from covariance_term_epsilon(), validate_analytic_epsilon(), and the new warning helper.

Add:

warn_direction_sensitivity(
  spec,
  term,
  epsilon,
  custom_means_pattern
)

The warning fires only when all of the following are true:

  • The caller is one of the balanced simulation-based APIs.
  • The tested term’s within-subject component has more than one degree of freedom.
  • epsilon < 1 - 1e-8.
  • custom_means_pattern is FALSE.

Suggested message:

This multi-df within-subject term is nonspherical, so simulated power depends
on the relative cell-mean pattern. `power_sim` currently uses the package's
default linear/Kronecker pattern; a different pattern with the same
`target_pes` and covariance can produce different power. Supply
`means_pattern` to describe the expected relative mean shape.

Hook the warning into prepare_balanced_power_inputs() after epsilon and the custom-pattern flag have been resolved. This single location covers power_curve(), power_n(), power_achieved(), and power_sensitivity(). Preserve the existing warn_ss_type_i_uncorrected_gg() behavior. With ss_type = "I", that warning and the direction warning describe separate real issues: the simulated p-value is uncorrected, and its nonspherical sampling distribution can still depend on the means direction.

Do not issue this warning for:

  • A supplied custom pattern: the simulation direction is then explicit.
  • power_n_calc(), power_achieved_calc(), or power_sensitivity_calc(): no runtime direction warning is wanted for the calculation-only API.
  • power_unbalanced(): its literal cell means already determine the effect direction.
  • A two-level within-subject component, whose within-term df is 1.
  • A purely between-subject term.
  • A spherical tested contrast space (epsilon == 1 within tolerance).

The warning should describe only power_sim. It must not claim that power_calc represents the package’s chosen mean direction; calculated power remains the conventional direction-insensitive noncentral-F approximation.

No informational message is needed when a custom pattern is supplied under sphericity.

Constructor implementation scope

Implement a small parser dedicated to means_pattern() in this pass. Do not refactor the existing cell_counts() and cell_design() parsers as part of this accuracy fix. Their row terminators and validation rules differ, and a shared-parser refactor would increase the regression surface without being required for the feature.

Documentation

Update:

  • Roxygen documentation for all five functions receiving the new argument.
  • A new help page for means_pattern().
  • The covariance vignette with a multi-df nonsphericity example comparing two patterns at identical target_pes and covariance.
  • The main README or getting-started vignette with a short explanation and a link to the detailed covariance discussion.
  • NEWS with the new constructor, arguments, projection semantics, and warning.

The documentation should distinguish clearly between:

  • target_pes: requested effect magnitude;
  • means_pattern: relative direction/shape of the target-term means;
  • covariance: residual covariance and nonsphericity;
  • power_sim: simulation power conditional on the selected pattern;
  • power_calc: the conventional direction-insensitive noncentral-F result.

The covariance vignette must answer explicitly: “If target_pes and the covariance are identical, how can power differ?” Explain that a scalar noncentrality summarizes aggregate signal magnitude, but under nonsphericity the actual univariate statistic has weighted noncentral components. Its power depends on the alignment of theta with the eigenspaces of Psi. GG correction changes the degrees of freedom and rejection threshold, but the underlying direction sensitivity is also present for the uncorrected univariate statistic. Include the already validated audit example verbatim, with its identical target_pes, covariance, epsilon, sample size, and alpha, and show the observed power_sim = 0.945 versus power_sim = 0.896 for the two mean directions. State that this difference is the motivation for means_pattern, not Monte Carlo instability.

The same vignette must document the normalized linear/Kronecker default, show the centered score vectors for two through five levels, and show the 2-by-2 and 2-by-4 interaction matrices. State that it is an ordered, reproducible convention rather than a neutral scientific assumption, and recommend an explicit pattern whenever the expected shape is known. It should also contain a short note comparing the three sparse-cell interfaces and explaining why balanced patterns accept indices or package-generated names while cell_design() can use user labels.

Calculation-only documentation may explain the conventional calculation, but the functions should not emit a new runtime direction warning.

Verification

Constructor and resolution tests

  • Accept sparse main-effect and interaction definitions.
  • Fill omitted raw cells with zero.
  • Broadcast across factors outside the tested term.
  • Reject unnamed values, incomplete rows, duplicate cells, non-finite values, invalid factor names, invalid generated labels, non-integer indices, and out-of-range indices.
  • Confirm that integer indices and their generated-name equivalents resolve to the same cells, and that mixed-form duplicates are rejected.
  • Reject factors not present in term.
  • Reject a raw pattern whose projection onto the requested term is zero, even if the raw values vary across cells.
  • Confirm that an interaction pattern containing nuisance main-effect components is projected successfully rather than rejected.
  • Confirm that the resolved matrix uses the package’s established between- and within-cell ordering.

Default linear/Kronecker tests

  • Confirm that the unnormalized centered linear scores are proportional to (-1, 1), (-1, 0, 1), (-3, -1, 1, 3), and (-2, -1, 0, 1, 2) for two through five levels.
  • Confirm that each normalized component vector sums to zero and has unit Euclidean norm.
  • Confirm that a 2-by-2 default is proportional to matrix(c(1, -1, -1, 1), 2, 2, byrow = TRUE) in the documented cell order.
  • Confirm that a 2-by-4 default is proportional to the documented matrix with rows (3, 1, -1, -3) and (-3, -1, 1, 3).
  • Confirm that two-way and higher-order defaults equal the Kronecker product of their normalized component vectors after alignment to the design grid.
  • Confirm that the final complete default pattern has unit norm, contains only the requested term component, and broadcasts correctly across factors outside the term.

Equivalence tests

Using the same seed and inputs, confirm that the following produce equivalent calibrated means and power results within numerical tolerance:

  • A pattern and a positive scalar multiple of it.
  • A pattern and its global sign reversal for omnibus power.
  • A pattern before and after adding an intercept or lower-order component.
  • Two distinct unit-norm directions in the same target-term subspace must produce the same calibration multiplier k for fixed target_pes, design, covariance, and sample size. Pin this invariant down directly around the calibration helper, and also verify equal calibrated mean-vector norms. This is the invariant that makes one scalar rescaling valid for every direction.

Existing behavior

  • With means_pattern = NULL, every balanced simulation uses the new normalized linear/Kronecker default. Tests must not preserve numerical output from the old internal sum-contrast direction where nonsphericity makes direction consequential.
  • Spherical power remains invariant to the default direction within Monte Carlo tolerance, although the generated calibrated means can change.
  • No legacy-pattern option is added; the affected development APIs are experimental.
  • The full existing test suite passes.
  • power_n(), power_curve(), power_achieved(), and power_sensitivity() all use the supplied pattern.
  • design_term_means() returns the calibrated matrix corresponding to the supplied relative pattern.

Sphericity check

Under a spherical covariance, compare the new linear/Kronecker default and another nondegenerate direction at the same target_pes. Their theoretical power should agree, and simulated power should agree within a prespecified Monte Carlo tolerance. Use paired seeds or common random-number draws where practical to reduce simulation noise.

Nonsphericity check

For a four-level within-subject factor with a deliberately nonspherical covariance, compare at least:

  1. The package-default pattern.
  2. A different explicit trend or curved pattern.
  3. A pattern dominated by one level.

At the same target_pes, covariance, epsilon, sample size, and alpha:

  • power_calc should be identical across patterns.
  • power_sim should differ when the directions align differently with the covariance eigenstructure.
  • Each calibrated mean matrix should attain the requested reference target_pes within numerical tolerance.

Run this comparison with the GG-corrected simulation path (ss_type = "III" or "II"). Also run distinct directions with ss_type = "I" and confirm that uncorrected simulated power can likewise differ under nonsphericity. Do not test equality merely because the scalar noncentral-F approximation is direction-insensitive.

Keep automated stochastic assertions tolerant enough to avoid flaky tests. Place a longer, higher-simulation validation in data-raw/ if necessary.

Independent validation

Cross-check representative patterns against an independently constructed multivariate-normal simulation using literal calibrated means and covariance. A Superpower comparison can be included as an additional development-time validation, but the automated correctness test should not rely exclusively on another package that may use similar ANOVA-fitting machinery.

Warning tests

Confirm that the new warning:

  • Fires for a balanced simulated multi-df within term with epsilon < 1 and no custom pattern.
  • Does not fire when a custom pattern is supplied.
  • Does not fire for a df-1 within term, a purely between term, or spherical covariance.
  • Fires for ss_type = "I" when the other direction-sensitivity conditions hold; the existing warn_ss_type_i_uncorrected_gg() warning also remains applicable because it describes a separate issue.
  • Does not fire from calculation-only functions.
  • Does not fire from power_unbalanced().
  • Refers to the conditional nature of power_sim and does not imply that power_calc uses the selected direction.

Primary files

  • R/power_curve.R: constructor, resolver/projection, calibration plumbing, warning helper, power_curve(), power_n(), and design_term_means().
  • R/power_effect.R: power_achieved() and power_sensitivity() plumbing.
  • R/within_covariance.R: shared within_term_df() helper.
  • R/power_n_calc.R: reuse the shared df helper only; no new warning.
  • R/print_methods.R: custom/default means-pattern line.
  • tests/testthat/: constructor, projection, warning, plumbing, and power regression tests.
  • vignettes/, README.md, NEWS.md, and generated help/site files: user-facing documentation.

Acceptance criteria

The work is complete when:

  1. Users can provide sparse relative mean shapes to every balanced simulation-based power workflow.
  2. Raw patterns are projected exactly onto the requested term and zero target projections fail clearly.
  3. Simulation power is conditional on either the explicit custom pattern or the documented normalized linear/Kronecker default.
  4. The hidden default produces a warning only for multi-df nonspherical balanced simulations, including the uncorrected ss_type = "I" path.
  5. Calculation-only and unbalanced workflows receive no new runtime direction warning.
  6. Spherical power is invariant to valid pattern direction within Monte Carlo tolerance, while nonspherical corrected and uncorrected simulations demonstrate the expected direction dependence.
  7. Distinct unit-norm directions share the same calibration multiplier for fixed design inputs.
  8. The default main-effect and interaction patterns match the documented centered-linear and Kronecker constructions and have unit norm.
  9. Existing tests and the package check pass.

On this page

Developed by Shaheed Azaad.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
